title: The connection between migraines and stored emotional tension
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
Your head is not the problem 

 I used to think migraines were just bad headaches. Something wrong with my brain. A chemical imbalance. A genetic lottery...
htmlContent:
Your head is not the problem

I used to think migraines were just bad headaches. Something wrong with my brain. A chemical imbalance. A genetic lottery I lost.

I spent years chasing the wrong answers. Neurologists. MRIs. Prescription medications that made me feel hollow. Nothing worked. Not really.

Then my body started telling me something I didn't want to hear. Every migraine had a pattern. Not just in timing, but in feeling. The tension would build in my shoulders first. Then my jaw would clench. Then the pressure would crawl up the back of my skull, settling behind my right eye like a tenant who refused to pay rent.

Here's the thing. Your body stores what your mind tries to forget.

Bessel van der Kolk spent decades studying this at the Trauma Research Foundation. He showed that trauma and chronic stress literally reshape the brain. They don't just live in your memories. They live in your muscles, your fascia, your nervous system. And for some of us, that stored tension finds its way to your head.

I'm not saying every migraine is caused by emotional tension. That would be reductive and dishonest. But I am saying that for many of us, the two are deeply connected. And ignoring that connection means we keep treating symptoms instead of causes.

Think about it. When was the last time you had a migraine during a genuinely peaceful period in your life? For me, the answer is never. My migraines always show up during or after emotional storms. After arguments I didn't fully process. After weeks of suppressing anger at work. After grief I tried to outrun.

Your head is not the problem. Your head is the messenger.

The vagus nerve and the tension trap

Stephen Porges developed Polyvagal Theory to explain how our nervous system responds to safety and danger. His work shows that when we feel threatened, our body goes into protective states. Fight, flight, or freeze. These states are not just psychological. They are physiological. Your muscles tighten. Your heart rate changes. Your digestion slows. And your head can become a pressure cooker.

I remember sitting in my car after a particularly hard therapy session. My jaw was so tight I could feel the ache radiating up into my temples. I had been holding that tension for weeks without realizing it. Months, maybe. My body had been screaming at me, and I was too busy surviving to listen.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium helps regulate the nervous system and relax muscles. It's not a cure. Nothing is a cure when you're dealing with complex trauma. But it can take the edge off. It can lower the baseline tension that makes migraines more likely.

The vagus nerve runs from your brainstem down through your neck, chest, and abdomen. It's the main highway of your parasympathetic nervous system. When it's working well, you can calm down after stress. When it's stuck, you stay in that tense, hypervigilant state. And your head pays the price.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets trapped in the body. He says that animals in the wild shake off their stress responses. They complete the cycle. Humans, on the other hand, suppress and store. We clench our jaws instead of roaring. We tighten our shoulders instead of running. We get migraines instead of feeling our feelings.

Stay with me here. This is not about blaming yourself for having migraines. This is about understanding that your body is doing exactly what it evolved to do. It's trying to protect you. It just doesn't know that the danger is over.

The neck, the shoulders, and the unspoken weight

I want you to notice something right now. Where is your jaw? Are your teeth touching? Is your tongue pressed against the roof of your mouth? How about your shoulders? Are they up near your ears?

Most people reading this just realized they've been holding tension they weren't aware of. That's not a coincidence. That's your nervous system doing its job.

Gabor Mate, who wrote extensively about the connection between stress and illness, says that many of our chronic conditions are expressions of suppressed emotion. He worked with patients who had autoimmune diseases, chronic pain, and yes, migraines. What he found was that the body often speaks what the mouth refuses to say.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Mate's work is not easy reading. It asks uncomfortable questions about how we live, how we cope, and what we sacrifice for the illusion of control. But if you've ever wondered why your body seems to rebel against you, this book offers a framework for understanding.

The neck and shoulders are particularly vulnerable to stored tension. Think about the posture of someone who is bracing for impact. Head forward. Shoulders hunched. This is the posture of someone who expects to be hurt. And if you grew up in an unpredictable environment, or if you're currently living with chronic stress, your body might be stuck in that posture permanently.

That tension compresses the nerves and blood vessels in your neck and upper back. It restricts blood flow to your head. It triggers muscle spasms that can turn into full-blown migraines. And the worst part is, you might not even feel the tension until the migraine is already building.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). I use mine on my upper back and shoulders. It hurts, honestly. But that hurt is the feeling of tension being released. It's the feeling of your body finally getting permission to let go.

Sensory overload and the migraine brain

Here's something I didn't understand for a long time. My nervous system was not just storing tension. It was also on high alert for threats. Every sound felt louder. Every light felt brighter. Every unexpected touch made me flinch.

This is common for people with complex trauma. Your nervous system learns to scan for danger constantly. And when you're in that state, your sensory processing threshold drops. Normal stimuli become overwhelming. And that overwhelm can trigger a migraine.

I used to think I was just sensitive. A delicate flower, as my father used to say, with contempt in his voice. But now I understand that my sensitivity was a survival adaptation. My body learned to detect threats early because early detection was the only way to stay safe.

The problem is that this adaptation outlives its usefulness. You don't need to scan for threats in a grocery store. But your nervous system doesn't know that. So it keeps scanning. And scanning. And scanning. Until your brain is exhausted and your head is pounding.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). I wear them in crowded spaces, during loud conversations, sometimes just when I need to quiet the world for a few minutes. They don't block everything. They just take the edge off. They give your nervous system a break from processing constant sensory input.

Wild, right? That something as simple as reducing noise can reduce migraine frequency? But it makes sense when you understand the nervous system. Your brain has limited resources. When it's spending most of those resources on threat detection and sensory processing, it has less capacity for regulation. Less capacity for repair. And more vulnerability to pain.

What actually helps (and what doesn't)

I'm going to be direct with you. The wellness industry has a lot of answers for migraines. Most of them are garbage. Essential oils, crystal healing, positive affirmations. These things might feel nice. They might provide temporary relief. But they don't address the underlying nervous system dysregulation that drives chronic migraines.

What helps is slow, boring, unglamorous work. Somatic therapy. Gentle movement. Learning to feel your feelings instead of suppressing them. Rebuilding your relationship with your body after years of treating it like an enemy.

What helps is understanding that your migraines are not a personal failure. They are a signal. A signal that something in your life, in your nervous system, in your history, needs attention.

I still get migraines. Less often than I used to. Less intensely. But they still come. And when they do, I try not to fight them. I lie down in a dark room. I put my hand on my head. I say, "I hear you. I'm listening."

That might sound ridiculous. But it's the most effective thing I've found. Because the opposite of suppression is not elimination. It's acknowledgment. It's letting your body know that its message has been received.

Your migraines are not your enemy. They are your body's desperate attempt to get your attention. And the only way out is through.
